message:
perf(btree): handle compact table-leaf delete via physical-neighbor scan

The table-leaf delete defragmenter had two paths:
  1) cell pointers in descending key order → recover the deleted
     cell's byte extent from `ptrs[delete_idx-1]`, then memmove
     content + adjust the surviving pointers.
  2) anything else → fall back to the slow "rebuild the page from
     scratch via the cell iterator" path, which decodes every cell
     to recover its physical layout.

The descending-order assumption holds right after a fresh insert
sweep but breaks the moment any UPDATE is applied: an UPDATE-in-
place that grows by even one byte forces the engine to re-emit the
cell into the free area, which leaves the cell pointer array
out-of-order even though the *physical* cell-content interval
remains contiguous. Once that happens, every subsequent DELETE
on the page falls into the slow rebuild path.

Add a third path between the two: when the page is small enough
that we can certify physical-content compactness with a linear
scan (`usable_size <= 4096`, which covers the SQLite default page
sizes 512/1024/2048/4096 we ship with), recover the deleted cell's
upper bound via a single linear pass over `ptrs[]` instead of by
key/logical order:

  - For each pointer, mark it in a 64-element `seen_offsets`
    bitmap (4096 / 64 = 64 u64 slots cover the worst case). A
    duplicate pointer flips the corruption check.
  - Track the *smallest* pointer that is strictly greater than the
    deleted pointer; that's `deleted_upper`. If no such pointer
    exists, `deleted_upper = usable_size`.
  - Validate `deleted_ptr < deleted_upper`; reject as corrupt
    otherwise.

The rest mirrors the descending-order branch: compute
`deleted_size = deleted_upper - deleted_ptr`, shift content
forward via `copy_within`, adjust every pointer that pointed
*before* the deleted cell by +deleted_size (with overflow checks),
zero the gap exposed at `[ptr_array_end..new_content_offset]`,
clear `first_freeblock` / `fragmented_free_bytes`, and rewrite the
page header.

Net effect: hot UPDATE-then-DELETE loops no longer regress to the
O(N·cell-decode) rebuild path. Pages stay defragmented in the
fast lane.

Also reorder the descending-order branch slightly so
`page_data.as_bytes_mut()` is reborrowed only after the validation
checks that don't need the mut borrow, matching the new path's
borrowing shape and avoiding an unnecessarily wide mutable scope.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
